instructor.name: Yen-Jen Wang
instructor.office_hours:
Wednesday 1:30 – 2:30 pm, and by appointment
To set up a meeting, please email me 24 hours before the meeting time and provide a few times that might work with your schedule. I will try to find a time that works for both. You should prepare questions before we meet.

chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Course Number: ISEN 302
Course Title: Economic Analysis of Engineering Projects
Section: 504 CRN 60361
Time: TR 5:30 pm - 6:20 pm
Location: Zach 342
Credit Hours: Credits 2 - 2 Lecture Hours

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content:
Principles of economic equivalence; time value of money; analysis of single and multiple investments;
comparison of alternatives; capital recovery and after-tax analysis of economic projects

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: MATH 152 or MATH 172 and ability to use Microsoft Excel.

chunks.title: Course Relationship to ABET Outcome
chunks.content:
2. An ability to apply engineering design to produce solutions that meet specified needs with
consideration of public health, safety, and welfare, as well as cultural, social, environmental, and
economic factors.
4. An ability to recognize ethical and professional responsibilities in engineering situations and
make informed judgments, which must consider the impact of engineering solutions in global,
economic, environmental, and societal contexts.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcome
chunks.content:
• Make engineering decisions based on an economic analysis of alternatives
• Explain and demonstrate the principles of economic equivalence and time value of money
• Formulate and solve equations using interest factors and formulas
• Distinguish between nominal and effective interest rates and convert between them
• Identify and determine constituent cash flows of single and/or multiple investments
• Examine, diagram, and compare investment alternatives on an economic basis using appropriate
terminology
• Conclude and justify the best investment alternative(s) based on appropriate analysis
• Develop and construct spreadsheet models to investigate alternatives
• Determine and utilize appropriate capital recovery methods for alternative

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
Engineering Economic Analysis (14th Edition)
D. Newnan, T. G. Eschenbach, J. P. Lavelle, and N. Lewis (February 19, 2019)
ISBN: 978-0-19-093191-9
Oxford University Press

Notes: This book was out of print for a while and while the bookstore indicates it is available again, it is
likely not in stock. A link to the e-book is provided, that is a more economical option that is
recommended.

https://www.vitalsource.com/products/engineering-economic-analysis-don-newnan-ted-eschenbach-
v9780190931940?term=9780190063467

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Homework assignments are due on the due date posted on Canvas. Please submit all work through
Canvas. You should submit your work on time. Late submissions are not accepted. In addition, there
are in-class assignments / checking for understanding, short quizzes, in-class quizzes, and three tests
during the semester. No make-up examinations will be allowed unless prior arrangements have been
made and only if you have a university accepted excuse.

The components of the course grade and their weights are: In-class Assignments, 5%; CFU / Quizzes,
10%; Homework, 10%; Test I, 25%; Test II, 25%; Test III, 25%.

The university letter grade policy is followed (>=90%: A; 80-89%: B; 70 – 79%: C; 60 – 69%: D; below
60%: F.)

chunks.title: Other Course Information Items (Course Website)
chunks.content:
The course website will be maintained through http://canvas.tamu.edu/. This site will contain
announcements and other information concerning the course. In addition, it will be used to distribute class
materials (homework assignment, notes,) and grades. Check the website regularly. Any information posted
there will be as valid as if it was mentioned in class.

chunks.title: Other Course Information Items (Individual Work and Citation)
chunks.content:
It is expected that all work submitted in this class will be done individually. If you got any help with
homework, please cite that person in your write-up. For example, "I thank John Smith for helping me
solve this problem." If you worked together with one or more other people, then each of you should cite
the other(s).

chunks.title: Other Course Information Items (FE Exam Calculators)
chunks.content:
A major reason for taking this class is to prepare all engineering majors for the Fundamentals of
Engineering (FE) exam, as well as to prepare Industrial Engineering majors for other ISEN courses. To
this end, students are strongly encouraged to use calculators that are approved by NCEES for use on the
FE. The NCEES website (http://ncees.org/exams/calculator-policy/) provides a list of the current
approved calculator brands and models.

chunks.title: Other Course Information Items (Exam Administration)
chunks.content:
Most exams are administered through Canvas using Respondus proctoring software. They will be
closed book/notes/smartphone. Calculators are allowed.

chunks.title: Artificial Intelligence Statement
chunks.content:
According to the Texas A&M University Definitions of Academic Misconduct, plagiarism is the
appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results or words without giving appropriate credit
(aggiehonor.tamu.edu). You should credit your use of anyone else's words, graphic images, or ideas using
standard citation styles. Artificial Intelligence (AI) text generators and natural language processing tools
(colloquially, chatbots - such as ChatGPT), audio, computer code, video, and image generators should not
be used for any work for this class without explicit permission of the instructor and appropriate
attribution. This includes, but is not limited to, Creating or revising drafts, editing your work, and
reviewing a peer's work
This excludes pre-existing software additions such as spelling and grammar checkers, which are
acceptable

chunks.title: Course Schedule (Dates are subject to change)
chunks.content:
Time durations for the topics are tentative and depend on class response.

| Week | Date | Topic |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | (01/12) | Introduction/ Ch. 1 Making Economic Decisions |
| 2 | (01/19) | Ch. 2 Engineering Costs and Estimating |
| 3 | (01/26) | Ch. 2 Engineering Costs and Estimating |
| 4 | (02/02) | Ch. 3 Interest and Equivalence |
| 5 | (02/09) | Ch. 4 Equivalence for Repeated Cash Flows / Exam. 1: Test Chapters 1 - 4 (02/12) |
| 6 | (02/16) | Ch. 5 Present Worth Analysis |
| 7 | (02/23) | Ch. 6 Annual Cash Flow Analysis |
| 8 | (03/02) | Ch. 7 Rate of Return Analysis |
| 9 | (03/09) | Spring Break - No school |
| 10 | (03/16) | Ch. 8 Choosing the Best Alternative / ABET Assignment (Due 03/22) |
| 11 | (03/23) | Exam. 2: Test Chapters 5 - 8 (03/24) |
| 12 | (03/30) | Ch. 9 Other Analysis Techniques |
| 13 | (04/06) | Ch. 11 Depreciation |
| 14 | (04/13) | Ch. 12 Income taxes for Corporations |
| 15 | (04/20) | Exam. 3: Test Chapters 9 - 12 (04/23) |
